title.en: Storage Accounts, Blob Tiers & Lifecycle Management

description.en: Design storage accounts like an enterprise: redundancy options from LRS to GZRS, hot/cool/cold/archive access tiers, lifecycle policies that move data automatically, and the data-protection features auditors ask about.

story_variants.en:
## The Most Deployed Resource in Azure

Almost every architecture diagram ends at a storage account: logs, backups, data lake files, static assets, Terraform state, ML training sets. Because storage is cheap *per gigabyte*, teams stop thinking about it — and then discover that petabytes of never-read logs sitting in the hot tier are a real line item. Enterprise storage design is three decisions made deliberately: **redundancy**, **access tier**, and **lifecycle**.

## Redundancy: How Many Copies, How Far Apart

Every blob is always stored as multiple copies; the question is where:

| Option | Copies | Survives | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| LRS | 3 in one datacenter | Disk/rack failure | Cheapest; a datacenter fire takes all copies |
| ZRS | 3 across availability zones | Datacenter loss | The modern production default in zoned regions |
| GRS | 3 local + 3 in paired region | Regional disaster | Secondary is not readable until failover |
| RA-GRS | GRS + read access to secondary | Regional disaster | Read-only secondary endpoint, eventual consistency |
| GZRS / RA-GZRS | ZRS locally + 3 in pair | Zone AND region loss | The belt-and-suspenders tier |

Choose by data class, not by habit: reproducible scratch data can be LRS; compliance archives usually justify GZRS. Remember that geo-failover is an explicit, hours-scale event with an RPO measured in minutes of possible data loss — geo-redundancy is disaster recovery, not high availability.

## Access Tiers: Same Blob, Different Economics

Blob storage offers four access tiers with one elegant trade: **storage price goes down as access price goes up**.

- **Hot** — highest per-GB storage rate, cheapest reads/writes. Data in active use.
- **Cool** — lower storage rate, higher transaction charges, 30-day minimum retention (deleting/moving earlier incurs an early-deletion charge).
- **Cold** — cheaper still, 90-day minimum, for rarely-touched data that must stay instantly readable.
- **Archive** — dramatically cheapest storage, but the blob goes **offline**: reading requires *rehydration*, which takes up to ~15 hours standard priority (an expedited 'high priority' option exists for small blobs, at a premium). 180-day minimum retention.

> **Callout:** Archive is a one-way airlock in the short term. Never archive data that a regulator, auditor, or incident responder might need *today*. The war story writes itself: a team archived security logs to save money, then a breach investigation needed them within the hour — and got them the next afternoon.

## Lifecycle Management: The Policy That Pays Rent

A **lifecycle management policy** is a JSON ruleset on the storage account that the platform evaluates roughly daily: 'blobs matching prefix `logs/`, not modified for 30 days → move to cool; 90 days → cold or archive; 365 days → delete.' Rules can also act on blob *versions* and snapshots, and can filter by blob index tags. Actions since 2024 also include `tierToCold`.

This single feature routinely cuts blob spend massively for log-heavy estates, because it converts a human process ("someone should clean that up") into physics. Combine with `last accessed time` tracking (opt-in) to tier by *reads* instead of writes when access patterns are unpredictable.

## Data Protection: The Checklist Auditors Read

- **Soft delete** for blobs and containers — a recycle bin with a retention window; the first thing that saves you after a bad script.
- **Blob versioning** — every overwrite keeps the prior version; pairs with lifecycle rules to expire old versions.
- **Immutability policies (WORM)** — time-based retention or legal hold; write-once-read-many for compliance regimes that demand tamper-proof records.
- **Encryption** — always on (SSE) with Microsoft-managed keys; switchable to customer-managed keys in Key Vault when policy requires; infrastructure (double) encryption for the truly paranoid.
- **Network posture** — disable public network access, use private endpoints (next module's topic), disallow account-key auth in favor of Entra ID where possible, and set `allowBlobPublicAccess = false` account-wide.

## Account-Level Choices That Bite Later

- **StorageV2 general-purpose** is the default kind; **Premium block blob** accounts trade higher storage cost for consistent low latency and high transaction rates.
- **Hierarchical namespace (ADLS Gen2)** turns the flat blob namespace into a real filesystem with directories and POSIX-ish ACLs — mandatory for serious analytics (Synapse/Fabric read it natively), but it must be enabled at creation and changes some API behaviors.
- Names are globally unique, 3–24 lowercase alphanumerics — generate them (`uniqueString` in Bicep), never hand-pick.
- Default max account capacity is measured in petabytes per account, but per-account ingress/egress throughput targets are finite: high-throughput pipelines often shard across accounts.

## Common Pitfalls

- Everything in hot forever — the default tier is hot, and nobody ever revisits it.
- Archiving data with a short-notice retrieval obligation.
- Forgetting minimum-retention charges: moving a blob to cool then deleting it a week later still bills the 30-day minimum.
- Enabling versioning without a lifecycle rule to expire versions — spend *doubles* quietly.
- Using account keys in app config when managed identity + RBAC data roles exist.

## Mental Model

Think of tiers as **temperature and distance**: hot data sits next to the CPU at premium rent; archive data is in a salt mine — safe, cheap, and slow to visit. Lifecycle policies are the conveyor belt between them, and your job is just to declare the schedule. Storage done well is invisible: correctly tiered, automatically expiring, immutably compliant — and never the surprise on the invoice or the bottleneck in the incident.

description.en: Lifecycle policies are the conveyor belt moving blobs down the temperature ladder as they age.

left.label: Security logs in archive tier — 15-hour rehydration during a breach

right.label: 90 days hot/cool for investigations, archive only past the response window

description.en: Archive is offline storage: match the tier to the worst-case retrieval deadline, not just the price.

explanation.en: Filters scope the rule — here only block blobs under the logs/ prefix are affected.
30 days without modification moves the blob to cool; note cool's 30-day minimum-retention billing.
Delete at 2 years turns retention policy into automation — no human cleanup job to forget.

lines: $ az storage blob show -c backups -n archive-2019.tar --query properties.{tier:accessTier,state:archiveStatus}
# standard-priority rehydration: allow up to ~15 hours

description.en: An archived blob being rehydrated — the state every runbook should anticipate before pointing at archive.
The full automated life of a log blob under one lifecycle rule.

instruction.en: Build a blob lifecycle cost estimator. Given BLOBS as (name, size_gb, age_days) tuples and hypothetical per-GB-month rates RATES = {'hot': 0.020, 'cool': 0.010, 'archive': 0.002}, implement tier_for(age_days) returning 'archive' if age >= 90, 'cool' if age >= 30, else 'hot'; and monthly_cost(size_gb, tier) returning size * rate. Print one line per blob in order: '<name> | <tier> | $<cost with 2 decimals>' and finally 'TOTAL | $<sum with 2 decimals>'. This is exactly the math behind a lifecycle-policy business case.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
app.log | hot | $1.00
backup.vhd | cool | $5.00
archive-2019.tar | archive | $2.00
TOTAL | $8.00

en: Check the age thresholds from largest to smallest: 90 first, then 30, else hot — order matters.
Format money with an f-string: f'${cost:.2f}' gives exactly two decimals with no trailing spaces.
Accumulate the total while looping, then print the TOTAL line after the loop — note the pipe separators have single spaces on each side.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert tier_for(5) == 'hot'
assert tier_for(30) == 'cool'
assert tier_for(89) == 'cool'
assert tier_for(90) == 'archive'
assert abs(monthly_cost(50, 'hot') - 1.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(monthly_cost(500, 'cool') - 5.0) < 1e-9
assert abs(monthly_cost(1000, 'archive') - 2.0) < 1e-9

q.en: What makes the archive tier fundamentally different from hot, cool, and cold?

en: Blobs are offline and must be rehydrated (up to ~15 hours standard) before reading
It stores only one copy of the data
It has no minimum retention period

q.en: Which redundancy option keeps three zone-separated copies locally AND three copies in the paired region?
A team enables blob versioning but no lifecycle rule for versions. The predictable outcome is...

en: Storage spend grows silently as every overwrite retains the old version

q.en: The cool tier's pricing trade-off is...

en: Lower storage rate, higher transaction charges plus a 30-day minimum retention
Free storage with per-read billing only
